one_line_takeaway: Large-scale masked-autoencoder pretraining (Face9M/FMAE) plus STRONG identity-adversarial regularization (large gradient-reversal lambda + linear identity head) removes the subject-identity shortcut in AU detection and sets new SOTA F1 on BP4D, BP4D+, and DISFA.
key_finding: A standard AU model (FMAE) memorizes subject identity — linear probing recovers identities at 83% accuracy — and this shortcut makes it converge fast (epoch 3) to a trivial, lower-F1 solution. Strong IAT suppresses recoverable identity (down to 27.9% linear-probe accuracy at epoch 19) and forces slower, progressive learning of the true AU decision boundary, improving F1. FMAE-IAT sets new state-of-the-art average F1: 67.1% on BP4D, 66.8% on BP4D+, 70.1% on DISFA. Regularization STRENGTH is the decisive factor: a small lambda=0.02 (as in prior work [87]) gives negligible gain, while lambda in {0.5,1,2,3} gives clear improvements, and a linear ID head (higher ID loss 0.152, larger adversarial gradient) beats 2-/3-layer MLP heads.
core_contribution: A data-centric and regularization-centric approach to Facial Action Unit (AU) detection with two own contributions: (1) Face9M, a curated 9-million-image facial dataset assembled from eight public face datasets, used to pretrain a Facial Masked Autoencoder (FMAE, a ViT trained with masked image modeling per He et al. [27]); and (2) an in-depth re-examination of Identity Adversarial Training (IAT) for AU detection. The authors show that AU benchmarks have so few subjects (each seen hundreds of times per epoch) that models take an identity 'shortcut', and they argue that STRONG identity regularization is needed to remove it. Their specific, novel design claims are that a large gradient-reversal scaler lambda and a LINEAR (1-layer) identity head together maximize the adversarial gradient magnitude and yield better AU solutions. Note on attribution: identity-based adversarial training was FIRST introduced by cited prior work Zhang et al. [87] (BMVC 2018); this paper's own contribution is the shortcut-learning analysis, the finding that [87]'s weak regularization (lambda in [0.008,0.08], 2-layer MLP head) is sub-optimal, and the strong-regularization design space. MAE itself [27] is cited, not their invention; FMAE is its application to Face9M.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a facial-behavior-understanding / representation-learning paper, not a security paper. 'Adversarial training' here denotes a gradient-reversal min-max objective used to make features identity-invariant for better generalization, not defense against adversarial-example attacks or forgery detection. No attacker, no threat model is defined in the paper, and none is invented here. (Tangential privacy relevance exists — IAT suppresses recoverable identity information in learned features — but the paper frames this purely as a generalization/shortcut-learning issue, not privacy or security.)
limitations: Reported by the authors: FMAE scaling on Face9M has not saturated even at ViT-large, so ViT-huge + distillation is left as future work; even under strong IAT the backbone retains some identity signal (27.9% >> 2.4% chance), which they attribute to non-face background cues correlated per subject. Reviewer-observed: AffectNet-8 FER used only 37,553 of 287,568 training images (cost), limiting comparability; lambda is dataset-specific (2.0 BP4D, 1.0 BP4D+, 0.5 DISFA), so some per-dataset tuning is required despite the claim it is easy to tune; gains on BP4D over prior SOTA MDHR are modest (FMAE 66.6% = MDHR 66.6%; IAT adds 0.5 point).

datasets_used: Face9M (proposed): 9M facial images curated from CelebA, FFHQ, VGGFace2, CASIA-WebFace, MegaFace, EDFace-Celeb-1M, UMDFaces, LAION-Face; images resized to 224x224
BP4D: 41 subjects, 328 clips, 140,000 annotated frames, 12 AUs, 3-fold subject-exclusive
DISFA: 27 subjects, 130,000 images, 8 of 12 AUs used (intensity >=2 = positive), 3-fold
AffectNet-8 (FER): 287,568 train / 4,000 test available, but only 37,553 Kaggle images used for training
ImageNet-1k: used only as a pretraining-source baseline for comparison against Face9M
quantitative_results: FMAE-IAT average F1: BP4D 67.1%, BP4D+ 66.8%, DISFA 70.1% (new SOTA)
FMAE (no IAT) average F1: BP4D 66.6%, BP4D+ 66.2%, DISFA 68.7%
FMAE FER accuracy: AffectNet-8 64.79%, RAF-DB 93.45% (new SOTA vs contrastive models)
Identity linear-probe accuracy on BP4D: FMAE up to 83%; FMAE-IAT 4.6% (epoch 1) to 27.9% (epoch 19); chance = 2.4%
Learning dynamics (BP4D+ fold-2): FMAE converges epoch 3 at F1 65.45%; FMAE-IAT converges epoch 15 at F1 66.66%
lambda ablation Table 6 (one fold): BP4D 68.81 -> 69.57 (+0.76 at lambda=2); BP4D+ 65.45 -> 66.66 (+1.21 at lambda=1); DISFA 71.06 -> 73.48 (+2.42 at lambda=0.5)
lambda sweep BP4D fold-2 Table 5: lambda=0 -> 68.33 (epoch 2); lambda=0.02 -> 68.60 (epoch 10); lambda=1 -> 69.26 (epoch 20); lambda=2 -> 69.57 (epoch 21); lambda=3 -> 69.47 (epoch 27)
ID-head depth ablation Table 7 (lambda=2): 1-layer 69.57 F1 / ID loss 0.152 / epoch 21; 2-layer 69.00 / 0.096 / epoch 7; 3-layer 68.90 / 0.085 / epoch 6
Per-dataset lambda used: BP4D 2.0, BP4D+ 1.0, DISFA 0.5
baselines_compared: AU detection SOTA: MDHR (CVPR'24, 66.6 BP4D / 66.2 DISFA), AUFormer (ECCV'24, 66.2 BP4D / 66.4 DISFA), GLTE-Net (65.7 BP4D+), CLEF (ICCV'23), MCM (WACV'24), ANFL, KSRL, PIAP, FAUT, SEV-Net, HMP-PS, KS, SACL, MFT/MFT*(depth), and GPT-4V (67.3 DISFA)
Generic encoders on BP4D+: ViT (61.6), CLIP (62.1)
FER contrastive/MIM models: LA-Net (64.54 AffectNet-8 / 91.78 RAF-DB), MCF, FaRL, CLEF, FRA
Pretraining-source baseline: FMAE pretrained on ImageNet-1k vs Face9M (Face9M always better at ViT-base and ViT-large)
Prior identity-adversarial method Zhang et al. [87] (BMVC'18): its weak setting (lambda in [0.008,0.08], 2-layer MLP ID head) shown inferior to this paper's strong regularization
